document.title: Command Deck Engineering: First Experiment
document.purpose: Define a minimal practical experiment for testing Command Deck Engineering with a small team, shared displays, and clearly defined roles in order to evaluate whether shared situational awareness improves software development coordination.

experiment-goal: Test whether a coordinated crew working around shared displays can construct software faster and with better shared understanding than traditional individual development workflows.
hypothesis: A coordinated crew operating with shared displays and defined roles can maintain shared situational awareness and accelerate software development.

mission.description: Build or extend a small software component while operating entirely within the Command Deck model.
candidate-projects: Extend the JSON Tree Editor to support arbitrary scripts.
Implement a visualizer for the Patchboard message flow
selection-criteria: Small enough to complete meaningful progress in 90 minutes

mission-selected.name: JSONEdit Extensible Menu System
mission-selected.objective: Add the ability to define new menu commands in JSON and export them for inclusion in the core distribution.
success-criteria: Menu items can be defined in JSON.
Menu items appear in the UI.
Menu definitions can be exported for distribution.

participants.operator: TBD - Wesley? - someone with Claude Code / Codex experience
participants.lead-visualizer: TBD - Max? - someone with computer graphics experience

captain.description: Directs system design and development decisions during the session.

first-officer.description: Constant architectural partner to the captain; participates in all reasoning and can assume command instantly if needed.
responsibilities: Acts as thinking partner to the captain during design discussions.
Maintains a parallel mental model of the system.
Takes over captain role temporarily if the captain steps away.

navigator.description: Maintains awareness of project progress and next possible actions.
responsibilities: Maintains a mission log of completed work.

operator.description: Executes commands on the system and operates automation and AI tools.
responsibilities: Operates AI tools when requested by the captain.

lead-visualizer.description: Translates system ideas into diagrams and maintains the architecture map.

librarian.description: Retrieves artifacts and information needed by the crew.
responsibilities: Finds documentation, code references, and specifications.
Projects artifacts onto the shared display when needed.

preparation: Board display connected to large screen
Map display opened with diagram workspace
Operator ready with terminal and AI tools

session-flow.description: Captain explains mission and system context
Crew iteratively designs, codes, and tests features

screen-1.name: board
screen-1.purpose: Primary shared focus surface used for active development, debugging, or discussion.

switching.method: Screen sharing through a shared meeting session (Zoom, Discord, etc.)
procedure: Participant begins screen share to take control of the board.
Board immediately switches to the new presenter.

screen-2.name: map
screen-2.purpose: Persistent architecture and system overview display used to maintain shared structural understanding.

switching.method: Visualizer maintains a persistent diagram workspace
procedure: Visualizer updates diagrams continuously during discussion.
Map display remains stable unless intentionally changed.

display-control-protocol: Captain may request a participant to take the board display.
Participant begins screen share to assume control.
When finished, participant stops sharing and control returns to neutral state.

workspace-layout.shared-displays: 1-2 large displays visible to the entire crew
workspace-layout.personal-workspaces: each participant uses their own laptop
workspace-layout.communication: participants remain in a shared voice call even if physically co-located

evaluation-criteria: Did the crew maintain shared situational awareness?
Did coordination occur faster than in typical development workflows?
Did misunderstandings resolve quickly through shared displays?
Did participants experience increased focus or engagement?
next-steps: perform a 10-minute dry run simulation of the roles
